howItWorks.intro: Drip irrigation delivers water slowly and directly to plant roots, reducing waste by 30-50% compared to sprinklers. Proper emitter spacing and run time depend on soil type, flow rate, and plant water needs.
rules: Emitter spacing depends on soil type: 12" for sand, 18" for loam, 24" for clay
Row spacing: 12" for sand, 18" for loam, 24" for clay (matches wetting pattern width)
Run time = (Weekly need × Area × 0.623) / (Total GPH × watering days)
0.623 gallons = 1 inch of water over 1 sq ft
howItWorks.outro: Water in the early morning (4-8 AM) to minimize evaporation. Split long run times into two sessions to prevent runoff, especially on slopes and clay soils.

faq.question: How far apart should drip emitters be?
faq.answer: Spacing depends on soil type. Sandy soil: 12 inches (water drains straight down). Loam: 18 inches (moderate spread). Clay: 24 inches (water spreads widely). These spacings ensure overlapping wetting patterns.

faq.question: What GPH emitter should I use?
faq.answer: 0.5-1.0 GPH for flowers and small plants in clay or loam. 1.0-2.0 GPH for vegetables and shrubs. 2.0-4.0 GPH for trees and large shrubs. Lower flow rates work better in clay to prevent runoff.

faq.question: How long should I run drip irrigation?
faq.answer: Run time varies by setup. A typical vegetable garden needs about 30-60 minutes per session, 3 times per week. The calculator determines exact run time based on your specific emitter count and flow rate.

faq.question: How much water does drip irrigation use?
faq.answer: Drip irrigation uses 20-50% less water than sprinklers because it delivers water directly to roots with minimal evaporation. A typical 200 sq ft garden zone uses about 120 gallons per week for 1 inch of water.

faq.question: Can I run too many emitters on one zone?
faq.answer: Yes. Your water supply has a maximum flow rate (GPM). Total zone GPH should not exceed your supply. A typical residential spigot provides 3-5 GPM (180-300 GPH). Divide large areas into multiple zones.
